data.title: More than 150 companies have ties to Israeli settlements, UN database finds
data.description:
Key points:
  • UN rights office lists companies linked to West Bank settlements
  • More than 60 new companies identified by UN since 2023
  • Report says they must ensure they are not contributing to abuses
  • Israel disputes UN court ruling that settlements are illegal

By Olivia Le Poidevin

More than 150 companies are doing business in Israeli settlements in the occupied West Bank that have been declared illegal by the United Nations' highest court, a report by the organisation's human rights office showed on Friday.

Israel, which cites biblical and historical ties to the West Bank, disputes the 2024 ruling by the U.N. court, arguing that the Palestinian territory is not occupied in legal terms because the land is disputed.

Most of the international community takes the U.N. view, and the human rights office said the 158 companies operating there - 68 of them added since the database was last updated in June 2023 - must ensure they were not contributing to rights abuses.

"Where business enterprises identify that they have caused or contributed to adverse human rights impacts, they should provide for or cooperate in remediation through appropriate processes," the report said.

Seven companies previously listed were taken off.

Most of the firms named in the database are domiciled in Israel but it also includes international firms listed in countries such as the United States, Canada, China, France, and Germany.

Scrutiny of companies' operations in the Israeli settlements has increased since Israel's military campaign in Gaza and simultaneous increase in raids in the West Bank that Israel says target militants but which have harmed civilians as well.

The list, which focuses primarily on business activities related to construction, real estate, mining and quarrying is not exhaustive, as the Office is still to screen more than 300 businesses submitted for assessment.

"This report underscores the due diligence responsibility of businesses working in contexts of conflict to ensure their activities do not contribute to human rights abuses," U.N. Human Rights Chief Volker Türk said. The report also said that states should act to ensure businesses were not contributing to abuses.

Israel and the U.S. have long protested the "disproportionate attention" given to Israel by the Geneva-based Human Rights Council. The Israeli embassy in Geneva did not immediately respond to a request for comment on the latest report.

Civil society groups say the database, mandated by the council in 2016, is an important tool to ensure transparency around business activities in the West Bank and to prompt companies to rethink their activities.

Israeli settlements have grown in size and number since Israel captured the West Bank in the 1967 war. They stretch deep into the territory with a system of roads and other infrastructure under Israeli control, further slicing up the land.

A U.N. Commission of Inquiry on Tuesday found that since October 2023, Israeli policies have demonstrated clear intent to forcibly transfer Palestinians, expand Jewish settlements, and annex the entire West Bank. Israel dismissed the report and said the Commission was pursuing a politically-driven agenda.

U.S. President Donald Trump said on Thursday that he would not allow Israel to annex the West Bank, rejecting calls from some far-right politicians in Israel who want to extend sovereignty over the area and snuff out hopes for a Palestinian state.

data.title: Crypto Treasury Stocks at Risk of 50% Crash After PIPE Deals — CryptoQuant Warns
data.description:
Crypto treasury companies that raised capital through private investment in public equity (PIPE) deals face a growing risk of their stock prices crashing by as much as 50%, to a new market report from analytics platform CryptoQuant.

The firm said PIPE-backed companies have already suffered steep declines, with share prices often gravitating toward their PIPE issuance levels.

From $35 to $1: PIPE deals are crushing Bitcoin treasury stocks.Kindly MD (NAKA) soared 18.5x in weeks, then fell 97%, back to its $1.12 PIPE price.PIPE price gravity is brutal. — CryptoQuant.com (@cryptoquant_com)

Researchers noted that when lock-up periods expire, investors frequently exit positions to secure profits, creating heavy selling pressure.PIPE Issuances Create Overhang Across Crypto Treasury Sector, Analysts Warn

PIPE deals allow private investors to buy new shares below market price, offering companies fast access to liquidity in a competitive sector.

While effective for raising cash, CryptoQuant warned that these arrangements dilute existing shareholders and leave an overhang of shares that weighs on stock performance.

One of the starkest examples is Kindly MD (NAKA), a medical company that pivoted into Bitcoin treasury holdings earlier this year.

Its stock surged from $1.80 in late April to nearly $35 by late May after announcing a PIPE raise. However, once PIPE shares unlocked, the stock collapsed 97%, falling to $1.16, almost identical to its PIPE offering price of $1.12.

CryptoQuant described the retracement as a case of “PIPE price gravity.”Source:

Other crypto treasury firms may now be following the same path. Strive Inc. (ASST) peaked at $13 in May but has since dropped 78% to $2.75.

CryptoQuant said its PIPE was priced at $1.35, implying the stock could face another 55% decline as investors prepare to sell when the lock-up ends next month.

Cantor Equity Partners (CEP), a special purpose acquisition company merging with Twenty One Capital, also conducted a PIPE priced at $10. CEP’s shares have already slipped nearly 70% from their highs to below $20.

CryptoQuant suggested the stock could fall another 50% from current levels once PIPE investors begin to sell.

The report noted that even well-established crypto treasuries are under pressure as the value of their digital asset holdings approaches parity with their overall company valuations.

This dynamic could further accelerate sell-offs if investors perceive limited upside in stock performance compared to direct crypto exposure.

CryptoQuant concluded that only a strong and sustained Bitcoin rally is likely to counteract the downward pressure facing treasury stocks tied to PIPE deals.

Without such a rebound, it warned that many companies are likely to continue trending toward or below their PIPE issuance levels.

At present, the trend has already left PIPE-funded crypto treasury stocks exposed to sharp losses, with investors closely watching how upcoming unlocks will impact market sentiment in the weeks ahead.Small-Cap Firms Turn to Borrowing as Crypto Treasury Strategy Falters

On the other hand, the crypto treasury strategy that surged across small-cap firms in 2024 is showing signs of strain as companies turn to debt-funded share buybacks to support collapsing valuations.

According to a CryptoNews report, at least seven firms, including gaming, biotech, and electric vehicle companies, have launched repurchase programs despite trading below the value of their crypto holdings.

Analysts say the trend reflects investor skepticism and undermines the premise that digital assets alone can boost stock performance.

📉 A quarter of all public companies holding Bitcoin now trade at market values below the worth of their BTC holdings. — Cryptonews.com (@cryptonews)

One high-profile case is ETHZilla, formerly 180 Life Sciences, which rebranded after buying ether. Its stock has fallen 76% since August.

The company recently secured $80 million in debt from Cumberland DRW to finance a $250 million buyback.

Critics argue the move signals distress. “They’re borrowing money to buy time, not tokens,” said Kaiko analyst Adam Morgan McCarthy.

Similar tactics are emerging elsewhere. Empery Digital, once Volcon, expanded its debt facility to $85 million despite holding $476 million in Bitcoin, more than its $378 million market value.

SharpLink Gaming, Ton Strategy, and CEA Industries are pursuing comparable measures.

Research from K33 shows one in four public Bitcoin treasuries now trade below their net asset value, with the average NAV multiple falling to 2.8 from 3.76 in April.

Smaller firms such as NAKA and Semler Scientific face mounting pressure, while larger players like MicroStrategy continue to command premiums.

The slowdown in BTC accumulation suggests the once-celebrated crypto treasury playbook may be nearing its limits.

data.title: These Stocks Are Moving the Most Today: Eli Lilly, Williams-Sonoma, Intel, Oracle, Paccar, Concentrix, Costco, and More — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Joe Woelfel and Elsa Ohlen

Stock futures were trading higher Friday following three days of losses for the major U.S. stock market indexes after inflation picked up in line with economists' expectations in August, leaving an opening for a possible interest rate cut next month by the Federal Reserve.

These stocks were poised to make moves Friday:

Eli Lilly rose 1.4% following President Donald Trump's announcement late Thursday that pharmaceutical companies would face a 100% tariff on branded or patented drugs unless they are currently building plants in the U.S. Lilly is one of many big pharma companies that lately have announced big investments in U.S. manufacturing. Other large pharmaceutical companies like AbbVie, Merck, Amgen, and Bristol Myers Squibb also were rising in premarket trading, while U.S.-listed shares of Danish drugmaker Novo Nordisk fell 0.7%.

Wayfair fell 1.7% after Trump's social media posts also said a 50% levy was coming on imported kitchen cabinets, bathroom vanities, and associated products, and a 30% tariff on upholstered furniture. Shares of peer furniture and kitchen appliances retailers RH and Williams-Sonoma were down 3.4% and 3.5%, respectively.

Shares of heavy-duty truck maker Paccar rose 6.2% after Trump also announced new tariffs on foreign-made heavy-duty trucks. Paccar makes Peterbilt and Kenworth trucks.

Costco Wholesale was down 1.4% even as the giant warehouse club reported fiscal fourth-quarter earnings of $5.87 a share, beating analysts' expectations of $5.80. However, while same-store sales, a measure of sales growth at stores open for more than a year and a key metric in retail, increased 5.7% from last year they were short of forecasts that called for an increase of 5.9%.

Intel rose 3.9% after the chip maker closed up 8.9% on Thursday to $33.99, following a report from Bloomberg that said Intel had approached Apple asking for an investment. Intel has risen nearly 40% in September and was on pace for its best month since January 1987, when it rose 49%, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Apple, meanwhile, declined 0.7% to $255. Analysts at Evercore ISI raised the firm's price target on Apple to $290 from $260 and maintained an Outperform rating on shares of the iPhone maker. Evercore said its annual survey of nearly 4,000 consumers around iPhone purchase intentions "points to what we believe is the start of better-than-expected iPhone refresh cycle, led by a strong lineup of products, particularly at the high-end."

Shares of Oracle were up 0.9% following a 5.6% drop Thursday as Trump signed an executive order to keep the social-media platform TikTok alive in the U.S., and confirmed Oracle's Larry Ellison would be involved in the deal. The stock has fallen for three consecutive days, declining more than 11% over the span. A Wall Street analyst on Thurasday cut his recommendation on the stock to Sell, arguing that investors have been "overestimating the value of Oracle's contracted cloud revenue." Shares remain up 75% for the year.

Concentrix tumbled 18% after the customer experience solutions and technology company posted fiscal third-quarter earnings that missed analysts' estimates, and issued a disappointing outlook for the current quarter. The company expects fiscal-year adjusted earnings of between $11.11 and $11.23 a share, down from a previous forecast of between $11.53 and $11.76.

CarMax was down 2% after shares tumbled 20% Thursday to $45.60 following weaker-than-expected quarterly earnings and a sharp decline in same-store unit sales from one of the largest U.S. used-car dealers. The drop Thursday was the stock's largest daily percentage decrease since Sept. 29, 2022, when it fell almost 25%, according to Dow Jones Market Data. It also was the stock's lowest close since March 20, 2020, when it finished at $44.27.

Write to Joe Woelfel at joseph.woelfel@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Treasury Yields Fall as Key Inflation Gauge Meets Forecast — Market Talk
data.description:
0849 ET - The Fed's preferred inflation gauge meets expectations and Treasury yields keep declining. August's 12-month PCE price index rises to 2.7% from 2.6%, with core steady at 2.9%. The indicator follows yesterday's surprising 2Q GDP growth upward revision and other signs of economic strength that could give the Fed reasons to be cautious about cutting interest rates amid sticky inflation. The University of Michigan consumer sentiment index, due at 10 a.m. ET, is forecast to cool. Next week brings labor data likely to influence the Fed's October decision. The 10-year is at 4.158% and the two-year at 3.647%. The WSJ Dollar Index is flat. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0838 ET - Canada may yet avoid back-to-back quarterly contractions but activity is far from booming. Industry-level gross domestic product rose 0.2% on-month in July thanks to a rebound in goods-producing industries. That was stronger than the 0.1% growth economists expected. Still, Statistics Canada estimates GDP was essentially unchanged in August as increases in wholesale and retail trade were offset by declined in mining and oil and gas extraction, manufacturing, and transportation and warehousing. The Bank of Canada is expecting growth of 1% annualized in the second half of 2025 after the economy shrank 1.6% in 2Q. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0818 ET - Total gross government bond issuance in the eurozone's 10 largest issuers is expected to rise in 2026 from 2025, to be led by higher German bond supply, Morgan Stanley's rates strategists say in a note. They estimate 2026 gross government bond issuance at around 1.43 trillion euros, up by around 80 billion euros or around 6% relative to 2025, they say. Supply pressures will remain more skewed toward core and semi-core countries relative to periphery, to be mostly attributable to higher borrowing needs coming from Germany, they say. Morgan Stanley strategists forecast slightly declining gross issuance in Spain, Portugal and Finland next year compared with this year. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0707 ET - A German business group representing chemical and pharmaceutical companies is urging the EU to push for an agreement with President Trump on tariffs for medicines. VCI says it is very concerned about Trump's plan to impose an 100% tariff on pharmaceutical imports as of October. The move comes after the U.S. and the EU struck a deal this summer for a 15% U.S. tariff on medicines coming from the bloc. VCI CEO Wolfgang Grosse Entrup says the tariff announcement is a low point for EU-U.S. trade relations. The U.S. is an important market for the German pharmaceutical industry, with exports amounting to 27.9 billion euros in 2024, he adds. The Stoxx Europe 600 Health Care index is up 0.1%. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0635 ET - South Korea's economy is set to slow in the coming quarters, which could lead to more interest-rate cuts, according to Capital Economics. Consumption is expected to hold up, buoyed by stronger consumer confidence and the pass-through of recent rate reductions, but consumer vouchers introduced in June will soon expire, removing a key stimulus, CE economists say. Construction investment remains weighed by a weak property market, and fiscal policy support is likely to be limited, they add. Despite strong semiconductor demand, overall exports will face pressure from U.S. tariffs and China's economic weakness. With subdued inflation and core price pressures likely contained, CE expects the BOK to deliver three more rate cuts, bringing the policy rate to 1.75%. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0632 ET - U.S. Treasury yields are little changed but are prone to volatility if PCE inflation data at 1230 GMT diverge from expectations, Sky Links Capital Group's Daniel Takieddine says in a note. Upcoming U.S. data will be pivotal in shaping expectations for U.S. interest rates after a week of cautious tone from Jerome Powell and resilient economic data, the co-founder and CEO says. "Both yields and the dollar could see renewed volatility if today's figures diverge meaningfully from expectations," he says. Investors are also cautious ahead of next week's U.S. monthly jobs data. The 10-year Tresaury yield edges down 0.3 basis points to 4.177%, having hit a three-week high of 4.201% on Thursday, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0630 ET - The cost of insuring U.K. sovereign debt against default using credit default swaps is at its highest since late April as concerns grow about the U.K.'s fiscal situation and debt sustainability. Yields on long-dated 30-year U.K. government bonds rise to their highest in three weeks as investors worry about the potential for higher borrowing and increased taxes at the upcoming autumn budget on Nov. 26. "The budget will need to deliver credible fiscal tightening, otherwise the U.K. risks testing investor confidence further," says eToro's Lale Akoner in a note. U.K. five-year credit default swaps rose to a five-month high of 22 basis points late Thursday and remain at that level Friday, S&P Global Market Intelligence data show. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0626 ET - Capital Economics forecasts Taiwan's economic growth to lose steam from this year's rate of around 7.5% but remain strong at 5.0% in 2026 and 3.0% in 2027, both above-consensus forecasts. The economists cite robust AI demand and rising domestic consumption and believe semiconductor exports will stay resilient despite U.S. tariffs. The AI boom is also likely to spur investment as firms expand capacity. Low inflation, faster real wage growth and upcoming cash handouts should revive consumer spending as well, they say. However, CE thinks fiscal and monetary stimulus may be limited, as President Lai's push to raise defense spending faces parliamentary resistance, while strong growth means the central bank is likely to keep interest rates on hold. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0608 ET - The dollar requires more good news on the U.S. economy to push higher again, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. Weak U.S. PCE data at 1230 GMT--the Federal Reserve's favored measure of inflation--could send the U.S. currency lower as its recent rally looks overdone, he says. The dollar rose to a three-week high against a basket of currencies on Thursday after a slew of strong U.S. data, including the final estimate of second-quarter GDP, jobless claims and durable goods. ING expects core PCE of 0.2% month-on-month, which could be enough to lift U.S. rate-cut expectations and weigh on the dollar, Pesole says. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.394 after hitting a high of 98.605 on Thursday. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0547 ET - Taiwan's decision to restrict chip exports to South Africa, which has since been paused, could alarm other countries, economists at Capital Economics say in a note. Taiwan suspended the restriction on Thursday, two days after the initial order, as the two sides agreed to discuss a dispute regarding Taiwan's representative office in the African country. "The threat raises questions about whether such curbs should now be considered part of Taiwan's armory in managing diplomatic ties," they say. Taiwan's dominant role in the semiconductor supply chain gives it some negotiating power, they say. Although Taiwan has paused the export ban, the perception that Taiwan might take advantage of its advanced chips for diplomatic goals could urge countries to reduce their dependence on Taiwanese chips in the future, the economists add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0535 ET - Indonesia's trade deal with the European Union isn't expected to provide much of a boost to the country's long-term prospects, economists at Capital Economics write in a note. The agreement, signed earlier this week, offered a respite for the Indonesian government, which has been on the defensive with investors over its policy direction. The economists note that the EU will remove tariffs on more than 90% of Indonesian goods following the deal's implementation. Palm oil and other commodity exports stand to benefit, as well as the textile sector. However, CE cautions that Indonesia's palm oil industry still faces risks from the EU's deforestation regulation, which could significantly disrupt exports to the bloc. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0516 ET - The U.S. dollar could struggle to rise much further as investors turn cautious ahead of next week's key jobs data, BlueBay Asset Management CIO Mark Dowding says in a note. The dollar rose to a three-week high against a basket of currencies on Thursday after a string of strong U.S. data. It had been on the verge of breaking out of its recent trading range but has since stalled. "This could infer further sideways price action in the short term," he says. Weak jobs data next week could give a renewed impetus towards a weaker dollar, which is in line with BlueBay's medium-term view. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.373, having hit a high of 98.605 on Thursday. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

"The threat raises questions about whether such curbs should now be considered part of Taiwan's armory in managing diplomatic ties," they say. Taiwan's dominant role in the semiconductor supply chain gives it some negotiating power, they say. Although Taiwan has paused the export ban, the perception that Taiwan might take advantage of its advanced chips for diplomatic goals could urge countries to reduce their dependence on Taiwanese chips in the future, the economists add. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0535 ET - Indonesia's trade deal with the European Union isn't expected to provide much of a boost to the country's long-term prospects, economists at Capital Economics write in a note. The agreement, signed earlier this week, offered a respite for the Indonesian government, which has been on the defensive with investors over its policy direction. The economists note that the EU will remove tariffs on more than 90% of Indonesian goods following the deal's implementation. Palm oil and other commodity exports stand to benefit, as well as the textile sector. However, CE cautions that Indonesia's palm oil industry still faces risks from the EU's deforestation regulation, which could significantly disrupt exports to the bloc. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0516 ET - The U.S. dollar could struggle to rise much further as investors turn cautious ahead of next week's key jobs data, BlueBay Asset Management CIO Mark Dowding says in a note. The dollar rose to a three-week high against a basket of currencies on Thursday after a string of strong U.S. data. It had been on the verge of breaking out of its recent trading range but has since stalled. "This could infer further sideways price action in the short term," he says. Weak jobs data next week could give a renewed impetus towards a weaker dollar, which is in line with BlueBay's medium-term view. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.373, having hit a high of 98.605 on Thursday. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Canada GDP Rises 0.2% in July
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--The Canadian economy grew more strongly than expected in July, buoyed by a recovery in goods-producing industries and an expansion in mining and oil and gas extraction.

Gross domestic product, a broad measure of goods and services produced across the economy, increased 0.2% from the month before to 2.292 trillion Canadian dollars, the equivalent of $1.644 trillion, Statistics Canada said Friday.

Economists were expecting GDP to have edged up 0.1%, in line with the data agency's earlier flash estimate. It follows 0.1% contractions in industry-level activity each month of the second quarter.

On a one-year basis, GDP expanded 0.9%.

The data agency said that advance information indicates GDP was essentially unchanged in August, though the estimate will be updated.

data.title: Poland to cut EU Recovery plan loans by $5.9 billion, minister says
data.description:
Poland will cut the amount of money it takes in cheap loans from the European Union's post COVID-19 Recovery and Resilience Facility by 21.5 billion zlotys ($5.87 billion), the Funds and Regional Policy Minister said on Friday.

Warsaw is grappling to bring its finances in check after two ratings agencies lowered Poland's outlook to negative, citing high deficits and debt as well as a lack of progress on fiscal consolidation.

It also does not have a lot of time to use the EU funds before an August 2026 deadline, as their disbursement was held up due to a row with Brussels over democratic standards under the previous government.

"We reviewed investments and demand for these investments, including for loans for companies, and the decision was made not to use these funds," Katarzyna Pelczynska-Nalecz told journalists.

Poland can receive nearly 60 billion euros ($70 billion) from the fund designed to help EU countries bounce back from the pandemic, made up of 25.3 billion euros in grants and 34.5 billion euros in loans.

With the time left to spend the money decreasing, Poland focused on making the most of the grants while treating the loans as a fallback, a senior official told Reuters last year.

Pelczynska-Nalecz said Poland would still use up the vast majority of the available loans.

The loans are classified as government debt even though they will be repaid by the final beneficiaries — such as businesses and local governments — over a longer time horizon.

Finance Minister Andrzej Domanski said last month that the public debt-to-GDP ratio will reach 66.8% by the end of 2026, but adjusting for the loan portion of the fund that would fall to 63.7%.

($1 = 3.6615 zlotys)

($1 = 0.8570 euros)

data.title: Canada Economy Stalls in August After First Growth in Four Months — Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Canada's economy grew for the first time in four months in July thanks to a rebound by goods producers, but activity looks to have since stalled.

Gross domestic product grew a modest 0.2% in July from the month before to 2.292 trillion Canadian dollars, the equivalent of $1.644 trillion, Statistics Canada said Friday.

That was slightly stronger than the 0.1% rise economists expected, following 0.1% contractions each month of the second quarter. All segments of Canada's goods-producing economy notched increases for the month, backed by an expansion in wholesale trade and real estate.

However, preliminary figures indicate industry-level GDP was essentially unchanged in August.

Canada's economy had widely been expected to recover from the hit earlier in the year from the abrupt shift in U.S. trade policy and ever-changing tariffs, but isn't expected to show more than tepid growth. The Bank of Canada, which this month resumed cutting interest rates in an effort to revive the economy and a weak labor market, has forecast growth of roughly 1% annualized in the second half of the year following a 1.6% contraction in the second quarter.

Statistics Canada said advance information indicated increases in wholesale and retail trade in August were offset by weakness across mining, oil and gas extraction, manufacturing, and transportation and warehousing. The preliminary data will be updated when official GDP figures are released at the end of October.

The data agency this week released advance figures suggesting the recent recovery by Canadian manufacturers and wholesalers, both of which have struggled amid heightened uncertainty and U.S. tariffs, faltered in August even as retail sales picked up as households continued to show resilience.

July's recovery in GDP, a broad measure of goods and services produced across the economy, was driven by a 0.6% month-over-month expansion by goods-producing industries following three consecutive months of decline. Services producers' activity edged up 0.1%.

Mining and quarrying led the monthly advance following back-to-back declines, while oil and gas extraction was buoyed by continued strength in the country's oil-sands industry as facilities ramped up production of synthetic crude oil and crude bitumen following maintenance in April and May.

Pipeline transportation also increased in July as exports of crude, bitumen and natural gas rose. Support activities for transportation also climbed thanks in part to the first full month of operation for the new liquefied natural gas facility LNG Canada on the west coast.

Real estate and rental and leasing activity in Canada grew for a fourth straight month as home resales have risen across the country following weakness early in the year.

Retail trade was the biggest weak area of the economy in July, tempering the economy's recovery, as a fall in sales partially offset growth in June.

data.title: FERRERO COMPLETES ACQUISITION OF WK KELLOGG CO
data.description:
Acquisition is the next chapter in Ferrero's proven strategy to acquire, 
invest in and grow iconic brands in North America, while also driving 
organic growth through brand innovation 
 
 
   LUXEMBOURG, Sept. 26, 2025 /PRNewswire/ -- The Ferrero Group ("Ferrero" 
or the "Company") today announced the successful completion of its 
acquisition of WK Kellogg Co. WK Kellogg Co is now a wholly owned 
subsidiary of Ferrero. 
 
 
   The transaction was previously announced on July 10, 2025, and approved 
by WK Kellogg Co shareowners on September 19, 2025. With the completion 
of the transaction, WK Kellogg Co shareowners are entitled to receive 
$23.00 in cash for each share of WK Kellogg Co common stock they owned 
immediately prior to the closing. WK Kellogg Co has ceased trading and 
will no longer be listed on the New York Stock Exchange. 
 
 
   The acquisition of WK Kellogg Co supports Ferrero's plan for strategic 
growth in North America and expands the Company's reach across more 
consumption occasions with brands beloved by consumers. Ferrero plans to 
invest in and grow WK Kellogg Co's iconic portfolio of brands across the 
United States, Canada and the Caribbean. 
 
 
   Lazard acted as lead financial advisor with BofA Securities acting as 
co-advisor and Davis Polk & Wardwell LLP acted as legal counsel to 
Ferrero. Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC and Morgan Stanley & Co. LLC acted as 
financial advisors and Kirkland & Ellis LLP served as legal counsel to 
WK Kellogg Co. 
 
 
   About Ferrero 
 
 
   Ferrero began its journey in the small town of Alba in Piedmont, Italy, 
in 1946 and has since grown into one of the largest sweet-packaged food 
companies, delighting people around the world with its beloved treats. 
Today, the Ferrero Group proudly offers more than 35 iconic brands 
available in over 170 countries. This is bolstered by Ferrero affiliated 
companies which include Ferrara, Nonni's, Fox's & Burton's Biscuits, 
Fine Biscuits Company, and Michel et Augustin. 
 
 
   Ferrero entered the US market in 1969 and today Ferrero Group and

and beloved local jewels including Nutella(R), Kinder(R), Tic Tac(R),

   In total, the more than 61,000 employees within the Ferrero family of 
companies are dedicated to helping people celebrate life's special 
moments with high-quality products and fulfilling the company's deep 
commitment to the planet and the communities in which it operates around 
the world. 
 
 
   For more information, visit www.ferrero.com 
 
 
   About WK Kellogg Co 
 
 
   At WK Kellogg Co, we bring our best to everyone, every day through our 
trusted foods and brands. Our journey began in 1894, when our founder 
W.K. Kellogg reimagined the future of food with the creation of Corn

data.title: India cuts ultra long bond supply for Oct-Mar, FY borrowing also dips
data.description:
By Nikunj Ohri and Dharamraj Dhutia

The Indian government has reduced the proportion of ultra-long bonds in its fiscal second half borrowing plan after suggestions across investor segments, shifting some borrowing to shorter duration papers.

The government will raise 6.77 trillion rupees ($76.32 billion) through market borrowings during the October to March period, a marginal 50 bln rupees lower than its previously announced schedule in an effort to tame rising bond yields and ensure market stability.

India had planned to raise 14.82 trillion rupees in the current financial year through March, and has sold bonds worth 7.95 trillion rupees in April-September, reducing the overall borrowing slated for this fiscal year to 14.72 trillion rupees.

Based on wider consultation with the central bank and market feedback, the share of long tenor securities has been reduced and the total borrowing for the years is now a shade lower than the initial estimates, economic affairs secretary Anuradha Thakur said.

"I would like to reemphasize that the government is committed to meeting the fiscal deficit target."

The government has reduced the share of ultra-long bonds which mature in 30 years, 40 years and 50 years to 29.5% in second half, down from 35% in first half.

The five-year and 10-year bonds will bear the brunt with an increase of 2 and 2.2 percentage point share respectively, while three-year bonds will see issuance go up by 1.3 percentage points.

Despite cancelling a 50-billion-rupee auction of green bonds in the first half, the government has scheduled 100 billion rupees of green bond selling for October-March.

Earlier this month Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman said "unsustainably" high bond yields are making government borrowing unaffordable when interest rates are otherwise low.

India's 10-year benchmark bond yield closed at 6.5231% on Friday.

($1 = 88.7110 Indian rupees)

data.title: Sanofi Expanding Insulin Savings Program to Insured Patients
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher

Sanofi is expanding its U.S. insulin savings program to include all U.S. patients who have a valid prescription, regardless of their insurance status.

Sanofi on Friday said the program will offer a 30-day supply of any of the French drugmaker's insulin for $35 starting to all U.S. patients on Jan. 1.

The company said the program, which it created in 2018 for people without health insurance, will soon be available to all Americans regardless of insurance status, including those with commercial insurance or Medicare.

Sanofi's insulin products include Admelog, Apidra, Lantus, Merilog, Soliqua and Toujeo.

data.title: Mexico Posts $1.94 Billion August Trade Deficit
data.description:
By Anthony Harrup

MEXICO CITY--Mexico registered a $1.94 billion trade deficit in August, smaller than the year-earlier deficit as exports rose and imports were slightly lower, statistics institute Inegi said Friday.

Exports increased 7.4% from the year-earlier month to $55.71 billion. Petroleum exports were down 26.3% at $1.64 billion. Crude oil exports by volume fell to 594,000 barrels a day from 769,000 barrels a day in the year-earlier month. The average price was $62.93 a barrel, down from $71.05 a barrel.

Exports of manufactured goods rose 9% to $51.71 billion, with a 1.2% decline in auto-sector exports and a 14.4% increase in other industries.

Imports in August were down 0.2% at $57.66 billion. Petroleum imports, which include natural gas and gasoline, fell by 14% to $3.87 billion. Imports of intermediate goods used in production processes rose 1.8% to $44.58 billion, and consumer goods imports excluding petroleum were down 0.2% at $7.26 billion.

In the first eight months of the year, Mexico had a trade deficit of $528 million, compared with a deficit of $17.98 billion in the first eight months of 2024.

Write to Anthony Harrup at anthony.harrup@wsj.com

data.title: enVVeno Medical (NASDAQ:NVNO) Participates in Virtual Investor "What This Means" Segment
data.description:
Watch the "What This Means" video here

IRVINE, CA / ACCESS Newswire / September 26, 2025 / enVVeno Medical Corporation ("enVVeno" or the "Company"), a company setting new standards of care for the treatment of deep venous disease, today announced that Robert Berman, Chief Executive Officer of enVVeno Medical participated in a Virtual Investor "What This Means" segment.

As part of the segment, Mr. Berman discussed the Company's recent news announcing it filed a request for supervisory appeal of the not-approvable letter from the Center for Devices and Radiological Health (CDRH) of the U.S. Food & Drug Administration (FDA) received on August 19, 2025, in response to its Premarket Approval (PMA) application for the VenoValve®, a surgical replacement venous valve for treating severe deep chronic venous insufficiency (CVI).

The Virtual Investor "What This Means" segment featuring enVVeno Medical is now available here.

About enVVeno Medical Corporation

enVVeno Medical is an Irvine, California-based, late clinical-stage medical device Company focused on the advancement of innovative bioprosthetic (tissue-based) solutions to improve the standard of care for the treatment of deep venous disease. The Company's lead product, the VenoValve®, is a first-in-class surgical replacement venous valve being developed for the treatment of severe deep Chronic Venous Insufficiency (CVI). The Company is also developing a non-surgical, transcatheter based replacement venous valve for the treatment of deep venous CVI called enVVe®. Both the VenoValve and enVVe are designed to act as one-way valves, to help assist in propelling blood up the leg, and back to the heart and lungs. The Company is currently performing the final testing necessary to seek IDE approval from the FDA to begin the U.S. pivotal trial for enVVe.

data.title: AstraZeneca to cut some direct-to-patient US drug prices after Trump demand
data.description:
Key points:
  • AstraZeneca offers diabetes, asthma drugs at up to 70% discount in US
  • Trump pressures drugmakers to lower US prices, link costs to other nations
  • AstraZeneca Direct website bypasses pharmacies, insurers for direct discounted sales to patients
  • Firm follows other drugmakers responding to Trump pressure

By Maggie Fick

AstraZeneca said on Friday it will sell its diabetes and asthma drugs direct to cash-paying U.S. patients at a discount of up to 70% off list prices, the latest pharma company to do so after pressure from U.S. President Donald Trump.

The Anglo-Swedish company, Britain’s biggest by market value, said uninsured or underinsured patients with prescriptions will be able to buy their Farxiga treatment for diabetes for $182 starting October 1.

That is the same price as Medicare and Medicaid patients will pay from January 1 next year, AstraZeneca said, and is 70% below the list price.

Asthma drug Airsupra will be sold for $249, about 50% below the list price, the company said.

Patients will also be able to order the nasal spray flu vaccine FluMist from the new AstraZeneca Direct website.

Farxiga is one of AstraZeneca's best-selling products, bringing in $7.7 billion in global sales in 2024, about 14% of total revenue. Airsupra made $66 million last year.

The company will ship the medicines to patients' homes, sidestepping pharmacies, insurers and the pharmacy benefit managers Trump accuses of ripping off Americans. It said in a statement that the new service is "an important step forward in offering patients the medication they need, when and how they need it".

It was not immediately clear how much patients would benefit from direct sales of these medicines. Reuters could not determine what percentage of patients taking the medicines are currently covered through commercial or Medicare health plans.

The new initiative aims to "address a genuine care gap for patients who have a prescription but cannot access Farxiga and Airsupra", an AstraZeneca spokesperson said, adding that the Direct website complements existing programmes that help a significant number of eligible patients access their prescribed medication at no or reduced costs.

TRUMP PRESSES FOR LOWER AMERICAN DRUG PRICES

In a July letter, Trump gave drugmakers until September 29 to propose binding commitments to cut U.S. prices to levels seen in other wealthy countries, having argued that Americans often pay up to three times more for medicines and that the U.S. has long been footing the bill for cheaper prices abroad. An executive order in May also called for more direct-to-consumer sales.

Trump on Thursday announced sweeping new import tariffs, including 100% duties on prescription drugs, set to come into force next week. However, major European pharma stocks were little changed on Friday, reflecting hopes that the 15% tariffs agreed in a U.S.-EU trade deal would hold.

DIRECT SALES

Other drugmakers are also scrambling to launch direct-sales programmes after talks with the Trump administration on affordability.

U.S. rivals Bristol Myers Squibb and Pfizer rolled out similar services in July for their blood thinner Eliquis, and on Thursday BMS announced another direct-to-consumer deal.

Drugmakers have not traditionally sold medicines directly to patients but Eli Lilly launched a service last year for its weight-loss drug Zepbound, and Novo Nordisk followed this year with one for Wegovy.

Insured patients typically make co-payments for branded drugs that vary from $25 to $75 or that are based on a percentage of the list price. Some plans require patients to cover the entire cost until they meet a deductible.

As part of its strategy to boost domestic production amid rising tariff pressure, AstraZeneca also recently announced a $50 billion U.S. investment plan by 2030 — including a new drug-substance manufacturing facility in Virginia, its largest single manufacturing facility investment globally.

data.title: Trump's Tax Deduction on Tips Is One Step Closer to Reality. Who Wins and Loses? — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kenneth Corbin

The proposal to reduce taxes on income earned through tips is one step closer to becoming reality.

Not all workers who receive tips are eligible, however, and some labor advocates argue that the proposal is a gimmick that diverts attention from the more urgent issue of raising the minimum wage.

The Treasury Department and IRS recently issued guidance on how the tax deduction would work. That provision, which became an oft-mentioned topic for then candidate Trump on the campaign trail, was included in the tax and spending bill the president signed into law on July 4.

The proposal would allow workers to deduct up to $25,000 of qualified tips from their income on their 2025 return. The deduction would be available both to filers who itemize their deductions and those who take the standardized deduction.

"[T]ens of millions of Americans depend on tips to feed their families, pay their rent, and make ends meet," says Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent. "All workers deserve an equal chance to pursue the American dream."

The deduction would begin phasing out for single workers with incomes over $150,000 and married couples earning more than $300,000. It would decrease by $100 for each $1,000 workers earn over those thresholds.

The proposal broadly includes workers in industries such as food and beverage service, hospitality, and transportation. The Treasury Department published a preliminary list of qualified occupations in August, which appears largely unchanged in the version published this month in the Federal Register.

But not all tip income would qualify for the deduction. It explicitly excludes tips received for any illegal activity and from prostitution services. The list of eligible jobs includes digital content creators, but the proposal excludes tips earned through pornography, which would shut out individuals who earn tips through a site such as OnlyFans.

The deduction only covers tips made in cash or a cash equivalent such as credit cards or checks that can easily be exchanged for cash. Other forms of compensation that don't have a ready cash conversion such as meals or many digital assets wouldn't qualify.

The proposal is limited to voluntary tips whose amount is "determined by the payor," meaning that the gratuities restaurants frequently include for large parties wouldn't qualify.

The Council of Economic Advisers has estimated that the provision will increase the average take-home pay for tipped workers by $1,300 a year. The deduction is set to expire in four years.

Interested parties will have a chance to make their voice heard on the proposal. The Treasury Department and IRS are collecting comments on the proposal through Oct. 22. The IRS is planning to hold a public hearing on the proposal Oct. 23.

Comments have already started coming in, many proposing technical adjustments to the guidance the IRS issued. David Fogel, a CPA in California, suggested that the agency shouldn't treat its list of eligible occupations as exhaustive, for example. He cautions that occupations not explicitly named in that list, such as a gas station attendant or garbage collector, might not be eligible for the deduction, despite collecting cash tips that would seem to match the spirit of the rule.

Some progressive groups, labor advocates, and economists of various stripes have panned the idea.

"'No taxes on tips' isn't a solution, it's a scam to keep wages for working people low while corporations walk away richer," the service-workers union SEIU has said. "Workers deserve fair pay, not empty promises."

The group, which didn't immediately respond to a request for comment, has argued that the deduction will yield the greatest benefit for higher earners, while lower-income workers who don't currently pay much or any income tax wouldn't see a significant change in their take-home pay. The Yale Budget Lab reports that 37% of tipped workers had incomes low enough in 2022 that they didn't owe any federal income tax.

SEIU has also warned that the policy could give employers license to rely more on tipped income for their employees rather than raising wages, which the union and others see as a more effective and broader-reaching policy for increasing take-home pay for low-income workers.

The progressive think tank Center for American Progress has argued that any benefits lower-income workers receive from the deduction for tipped income will be offset — and then some — by cuts to Medicare and other programs in the tax and spending bill.

The Tax Foundation, a think tank that often advocates tax policies embraced by conservatives, suggests that the deduction could lead to more businesses encouraging customers to tip on transactions. "This kind of behavioral response makes it difficult to estimate the cost of the no-tax-on-tips proposal," says Alex Muresianu, a senior policy analyst with the group. If the policy were to stay in effect for 10 years, he offers a lower-bound estimate of the shortfall in tax revenue at $107 billion. The Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget puts that number at between $150 billion and $250 billion.

Write to advisor.editors@barrons.com

data.title: Canada Wholesale Sales Estimated to Have Fallen 1.3% in August
data.description:
Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--Canadian wholesale trade pulled back from a recent recovery in August, with an advance estimate pointing to the first decline in sales in four months.

Statistics Canada on Friday said an early tally of wholesale receipts points to a 1.3% decline in sales from the month before.

That adds to data earlier in the week showing the recovery by factories in the country had also floundered, and that the economy appears to have flatlined in August after seeing modest growth the month before, thanks to a rebound in goods-producing industries.

Statistics Canada said the advance wholesale results indicate lower sales in the motor vehicle and vehicle parts and accessories industry.

The estimate was based on a weighted response rate to its monthly survey of 72.9%. Statistics Canada is scheduled to release official wholesale trade data Oct. 15.

Sales in Canada's wholesale sector rose 1.2% between June and July, driven by increased trade in motor vehicles and parts and building materials and supplies.

Earlier in the week, Statistics Canada said manufacturing sales declined by an estimated 1.5% in August, compared with a recovery for the sector the previous two months. Still, its advance look at retail trade pointed to a 1.0% increase in sales from the month before.

Manufacturing and wholesale sales have been hit hard by uncertainty following the Trump administration's shift in trade policy and ever-changing tariffs.

Statistics Canada estimated industry-level gross domestic product was essentially unchanged in August after expanding 0.2% on a month earlier in July.

The economy contracted 1.6% on an annualized basis in the second quarter.

The Bank of Canada, which this month resumed cutting interest rates, anticipates annualized growth of 1% in the second half of the year.

Wholesalers--the largest component of Canada's services sector--connect farmers or manufacturers that produce goods with the companies and public institutions that use them. They also import goods from other countries and redistribute them within Canada. Statistics Canada's wholesale measure excludes petroleum and oilseed and grain sales.

Write to Robb M. Stewart at robb.stewart@wsj.com

data.title: US Consumer Spending Rises More than Expected
data.description:
Personal spending in the United States increased by 0.6% from the previous month in August of 2025, picking up from 0.5% in July and above market expectations of 0.5%.

It was the sharpest increase in personal spending in five months, extending the period of resilience from the US consumer despite heightened economic uncertainty and elevated borrowing costs.

data.title: Lockheed Martin targets European market for Thaad missile defence
data.description:
U.S. defence contractor Lockheed Martin is seeking to market its Thaad missile defence system to European buyers, the company told German newspaper Handelsblatt, as governments across the continent accelerate military spending in response to Russian threats.

In an interview published on Friday, the president of Lockheed Martin International, Michael Williamson, said the group is in negotiations with potential customers for the system, which costs more than 1 billion euros ($1.17 billion).

Thaad could be introduced to Europe as part of the European Skyshield Initiative, an air-defence project led by Germany, as an alternative to Israel's Arrow 3 system, for which Berlin has already opted, the paper reported.

DRONE PARTNERSHIPS RESHAPE MILITARY PROCUREMENT

Europe's defence budgets have soared since Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022, with governments including Germany planning hundreds of billions of euros in investments.

Recent incidents involving suspected Russian drones entering NATO airspace, including in Denmark and Poland, have added urgency to bolster missile and drone defences.

This has attracted Northrop Grumman and Lockheed Martin to compete in the region, according to Handelsblatt. Williamson told the paper he sees the greatest growth prospects in markets outside the U.S., such as Europe, over the next five years.

Lockheed Martin's partnership with Rheinmetall is seen as key to securing future contracts in Germany. Williamson said the cooperation creates jobs and opens doors to political decision-makers, according to Handelsblatt.

However, European governments remain wary of U.S. dominance, with Denmark recently rejecting the U.S. Patriot system in favour of the European-made SAMP/T.

($1 = 0.8565 euros)
